perturbation.original_text: With respect to each year of such Facility's Warranty Period, the Service Fees shall be invoiced not later than thirty (30) days prior to the end of the calendar month in which the anniversary of the date of Commencement of Operations for such Bloom System occurred, and shall be payable within thirty (30) days of invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to each year of such Facility's Warranty Period, the Service Fees shall be invoiced not later than seventy-five (75) days prior to the end of the calendar month in which the anniversary of the date of Commencement of Operations for such Bloom System occurred, and shall be payable within sixty (60) days of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: The change in invoicing and payment terms introduces a potential inconsistency, as extending the payment timeframe may violate prompt payment laws in some jurisdictions if these services are considered construction-related. The original text was already fairly generous, and it's not clear why the invoicing date was extended so far in advance.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York State Finance Law § 139-f mandates specific prompt payment terms for state agencies and contractors. While this contract isn't directly with a NY state agency, the presence of 'Governing Law, Jurisdiction, Venue' clause referencing New York law and courts (Section 9.6) and the provision that 'THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND INTERPRETED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK' creates an argument that NY law should be considered, particularly if services under this agreement could be construed as related to construction or infrastructure projects. Extending the payment timeframe to 60 days from invoice introduces a potential conflict if a court determined the services fell under prompt payment requirements usually intended to be paid within 30 days.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment terms, extending the payment timeframe to 60 days, while the original text was 30 days. The Law Explanation suggests this change introduces a potential conflict with New York State Finance Law § 139-f if the services are construed as construction-related. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly specify the prompt payment terms, it references 'ARTICLE 9 Contracts' within 'CHAPTER 56 State Finance,' indicating the existence of contractual obligations governed by New York State Finance Law. The changed text creates an inconsistency by potentially violating prompt payment requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Thereafter, a meeting shall be held promptly between the Parties, attended by representatives of the Parties with decision-making authority regarding the dispute, to attempt in good faith to negotiate a resolution of the dispute. If the Parties are not successful in resolving a dispute within twenty-one (21) days, then, subject to the limitations on remedies set forth in Section 4.1 and Article 7, either Party may pursue whatever rights it has available under this Agreement, at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Thereafter, a meeting shall be held at the convenience of Operator between the Parties, attended by representatives of the Parties without independent decision-making authority regarding the dispute, to attempt in good faith to negotiate a resolution of the dispute. If the Parties are not successful in resolving a dispute within ninety (90) days, then, subject to the limitations on remedies set forth in Section 4.1 and Article 7, either Party may pursue whatever rights it has available under this Agreement, at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: This change increases the dispute resolution period to 90 days and removes independent decision-making authority, this impacts the parties ability to seek remedies for breach of contract, and extends dispute resolution beyond reasonable timeframes, contradicting principles of efficient dispute resolution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 7503
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 7503 outlines procedures for compelling or staying arbitration. While this agreement does not mandate arbitration, it does specify a dispute resolution process. By extending the resolution timeframe to 90 days and removing independent decision-making authority, the revised text unreasonably delays access to legal remedies, potentially conflicting with the spirit of CPLR § 7503 and general principles of timely dispute resolution. Delaying dispute resolution impacts the claimant and delaying their ability to seek appropriate remedies for breach of contract.

Application to compel or stay arbitration; stay of action; notice of intention to arbitrate. (a) Application to compel arbitration; stay of action. A party aggrieved by the failure of another to arbitrate may apply for an order compelling arbitration. Where there is no substantial question whether a valid agreement was made or complied with, and the claim sought to be arbitrated is not barred by limitation under subdivision (b) of section 7502, the court shall direct the parties to arbitrate. Where any such question is raised, it shall be tried forthwith in said court. If an issue claimed to be arbitrable is involved in an action pending in a court having jurisdiction to hear a motion to compel arbitration, the application shall be made by motion in that action. If the application is granted, the order shall operate to stay a pending or subsequent action, or so much of it as is referable to arbitration. (b) Application to stay arbitration. Subject to the provisions of subdivision (c), a party who has not participated in the arbitration and who has not made or been served with an application to compel arbitration, may apply to stay arbitration on the ground that a valid agreement was not made or has not been complied with or that the claim sought to be arbitrated is barred by limitation under subdivision (b) of section 7502. (c) Notice of intention to arbitrate. A party may serve upon another party a demand for arbitration or a notice of intention to arbitrate, specifying the agreement pursuant to which arbitration is sought and the name and address of the party serving the notice, or of an officer or agent thereof if such party is an association or corporation, and stating that unless the party served applies to stay the arbitration within twenty days after such service he shall thereafter be precluded from objec
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the dispute resolution timeframe to 90 days and removes independent decision-making authority. This change conflicts with the principles of timely dispute resolution which impacts the claimant's ability to seek remedies for breach of contract. The Law Explanation states that this unreasonably delays access to legal remedies, potentially conflicting with the spirit of CPLR § 7503. Scraped Law Snippet 1 outlines procedures for compelling or staying arbitration. While the contract doesn't mandate arbitration, the delay in resolution impacts the claimant and delaying their ability to seek appropriate remedies for breach of contract.

perturbation.original_text: Operator shall cause Owner (and such additional parties as Owner designates in writing) to be named additional insured(s), must be written as primary policy not contributing to or in excess of any policies carried by the Owner, and each contain a waiver of subrogation endorsement, in form and substance reasonably satisfactory to the Owner, in favor of the Owner.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator shall use commercially reasonable efforts to cause Owner to be named as an additional insured; however, the policy will be excess to any policies carried by the Owner, and a waiver of subrogation will not be provided.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the insurance protection for the Owner, by removing the primary coverage requirement, providing that its policy is in excess of owner's and eliminating the waiver of subrogation. This creates potential non-compliance with standard contractual insurance requirements and could contradict statutory requirements depending on the relationship between the parties and the nature of the work being performed.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Workers' Compensation Law § 29(1-a) addresses situations where an employee injured on the job can sue a third party. A waiver of subrogation prevents the insurance company from seeking to recover payments they made to the injured worker from the Owner. By removing the requirement for a waiver of subrogation, the revised text potentially allows the insurance carrier to subrogate against Owner in the event of a worker injury, increasing Owner's liability. While not a direct violation, it undermines typical risk allocation and could conflict with interpretations of the law seeking to protect employers from unexpected liabilities in work-related injuries.

Remedies of employees; subrogation. 1. If an employee entitled to compensation under this chapter be injured or killed by the negligence or wrong of another not in the same employ, such injured employee, or in case of death, his dependents, need not elect whether to take compensation and medical benefits under this chapter or to pursue his remedy against such other but may take such compensation and medical benefits and at any time either prior thereto or within six months after the awarding of compensation or within nine months after the enactment of a law or laws creating, establishing or affording a new or additional remedy or remedies, pursue his remedy against such other subject to the provisions of this chapter. If such injured employee, or in case of death, his dependents, take or intend to take compensation, and medical benefits in the case of an employee, under this chapter and desire to bring action against such other, such action must be commenced not later than six months after the awarding of compensation or not later than nine months after the enactment of such law or laws creating, establishing or affording a new or additional remedy or remedies and in any event before the expiration of one year from the date such action accrues. In such case, the state insurance fund, if compensation be payable therefrom, and otherwise the person, association, corporation or insurance carrier liable for the payment of such compensation, as the case may be, shall have a lien on the proceeds of any recovery from such other, whether by judgment, settlement
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for a waiver of subrogation. The Law Explanation states that NY Workers' Compensation Law § 29(1-a) addresses situations where an employee injured on the job can sue a third party, and that a waiver of subrogation prevents the insurance company from seeking to recover payments from the Owner. The original contract required a waiver, while the changed version explicitly states that a waiver of subrogation will not be provided. This contradicts the typical risk allocation under Workers Compensation Law.
